one_line_takeaway: Adversarially framed PR metadata (not new code, but persuasive commit messages/descriptions crafted via an iterative LLM-vs-ACR refinement loop) drove Claude-Code-based automated security code review to approve reintroduction of 17/17 previously-fixed real-world CVEs, while simple redaction of PR metadata restored correct detection in 16/17 cases.
key_finding: Exploratory study: bug-free framing produces the strongest detection degradation and is asymmetric relative to bug-present framing. Under strong bug-free framing, detection rate on vulnerable code drops from a 97.2% neutral baseline to 3.6% for GPT-4o-mini (a 93.5 percentage-point decline), with significant drops also for Claude 3.5 Haiku (-59.9pp), DeepSeek V3 (-42.9pp), Sonnet 4.5 (-16.7pp), and Gemini 2.0 Flash (-16.2pp); Opus 4.5 was the only model with a non-significant degradation (-4.9pp). All models also show very high baseline false-positive rates on patched code even under neutral framing (>88%, except Claude 3.5 Haiku at 68.4%). Attack evaluation: template-based attacks added zero acceptances beyond the unbiased baseline (16/17 baseline rejections stayed rejected across all 136 template permutations) and in some cases backfired by drawing scrutiny. The LLM-assisted iterative attack achieved a single-attempt (Biased-1) success rate of 9/17 (53%), and after iterative refinement (Biased-2, up to 5 iterations for the remaining 8 cases) reached a cumulative 100% success rate (17/17); 82% of cases (14/17) succeeded within two iterations, at a total measured API cost of $19.36 across all 17 attacks. Redacting the PR description alone (Debiased-1) restored correct detection in 12/17 (70%) of cases; adding an explicit instruction to ignore commit metadata (Debiased-2) recovered 4 more, for an overall 16/17 (94%) detection-recovery rate -- with the single remaining case (mcp/python-sdk, CVE-2025-66416) affecting only a non-recommended deployment configuration and therefore arguably not a true miss.
core_contribution: The paper studies whether the LLM cognitive framing effect (presentation of information overriding semantic content) is an exploitable attack surface in LLM-based Automated Code Review (ACR) systems used for security review of pull requests. It makes three own contributions: (1) a controlled exploratory study across 6 LLMs (GPT-4o-mini, Claude 3.5 Haiku, Gemini 2.0 Flash, DeepSeek V3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Claude Opus 4.5) under 5 framing conditions on 497 CrossVul files (14,910 queries total), establishing that framing systematically shifts vulnerability-detection judgments, with bug-free framing producing the largest effect; (2) an original threat model and attack, 'contextual-bias injection,' in which an adversary reverts a known CVE fix and crafts PR metadata (commit message, PR description, code comments) to frame the revert as benign or security-improving, tested end-to-end against real, deployed Claude-Code-based ACR pipelines on 17 CVEs across 10 real-world GitHub projects (314-71.1k stars); (3) two attack strategies -- a template-based attack adapted from Moon et al.'s bias templates, and a novel LLM-assisted iterative refinement attack that resubmits the PR using ACR rejection feedback -- plus an evaluation of redaction-based debiasing defenses.
threat_model: Adversary with normal PR-submission access to an open-source repository whose CI pipeline runs an autonomous LLM-based ACR agent (Claude Code via claude-code-action). The adversary does not invent new vulnerabilities; they identify a previously fixed CVE from public commit history/NVD, compute a clean git-revert of the fixing commit (or an agent-assisted faithful revert if a clean revert is not possible), and submit it as a PR whose title/commit message/PR description/code comments are crafted to frame the revert as benign, a performance optimization, or itself a security hardening. The core exploited asymmetry: the attacker can test and iteratively refine metadata against a locally cloned copy of the review pipeline before submission (in the paper's setup, up to t=10 iterations), while the defending ACR agent sees only the single, polished, final submission and has no visibility into rejected prior attempts.
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: (1) Generalization -- results target Claude Code specifically as deployed via claude-code-action; transfer to other ACR tools (CodeRabbit, Greptile) is untested and left to future work; most studied projects use Sonnet 4.5 (three use Opus 4.5), and although the 100% attack success rate against the flagship model suggests weaker models are unlikely to be more robust, this is inference, not direct evidence. (2) Internal validity -- Claude Code is run locally rather than via actual GitHub Actions to avoid touching live repos, so the review agent lacks GitHub-native context (issue trackers, prior PR discussion threads), partially mitigated by full repo history access and web search. (3) Sample size -- per-project manual setup (forking, prompt extraction, supervised tool-use approval) constrained the dataset to 17 CVEs across 10 projects. (4) The attacker uses the same LLM as the ACR pipeline in all experiments; different attacker/defender model pairings are left unexamined. (5) The paper does not test contextual-bias injection against human reviewers, so claims about attacker advantage are relative to ACR only, by the authors' own admission.

datasets_used: CrossVul (27,476 files / 13,738 vulnerable-fixed pairs; filtered to 3,968 single-file, <100k-token pairs; stratified sample of 250 pairs / 497 files across 5 CWE-language combinations: CWE-79 PHP, CWE-79 JS, CWE-89 PHP, CWE-125 C, CWE-787 C) for the exploratory framing study

Custom real-world dataset of 17 CVEs across 10 GitHub projects (strapi, xbmc, cherry-studio, mlflow, shakapacker, local-deep-research, typebot.io, WordPress-Simple-History, whodb, mcp/python-sdk), 314-71.1k GitHub stars, TypeScript/C++/Python/Go/Ruby/JavaScript/PHP, for the attack evaluation
quantitative_results: Exploratory study: GPT-4o-mini detection on vulnerable code drops 97.2% (neutral) to 3.6% (strong bug-free framing), a 93.5pp decline
Claude 3.5 Haiku: -59.9pp under strong bug-free framing; DeepSeek V3: -42.9pp; Claude Sonnet 4.5: -16.7pp; Gemini 2.0 Flash: -16.2pp; Claude Opus 4.5: -4.9pp (only non-significant degradation)
Baseline (neutral) false-positive rate on patched code >88% for all models except Claude 3.5 Haiku (68.4%)
Manual validation: incorrect justifications on correct vulnerable-code detections ranged 57.6% (Gemini 2.0 Flash) to 71.0% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) under neutral framing
Template-based attack: 0/136 permutations (8 combinations x 17 CVEs) flipped any of the 16 baseline rejections to acceptance
LLM-assisted attack Biased-1 (single attempt): 9/17 (53%) accepted, 4/17 (24%) unclear, 4/17 (24%) rejected
LLM-assisted attack Biased-2 (iterative refinement, up to 10 iterations): cumulative 17/17 (100%) success; 82% (14/17) succeeded within 2 iterations; total cost $19.36 across all 17 attacks
Debiased-1 (redact PR description): recovers detection in 12/17 (70%) of successful-attack cases
Debiased-2 (redact description + instruct agent to ignore metadata): recovers 4 additional cases, raising overall detection to 16/17 (94%)
Bias effect observed overall in 16/17 (94%) of cases
baselines_compared: Neutral-framing baseline (no security framing) as the reference condition in the exploratory study
Unbiased 'Change {filenames}' PR (baseline attack) yielding 16/17 (94%) rejections in the attack evaluation

Template-based bias attack adapted from Moon et al. [49]'s LLM-judge bias categories (authority, self-correctness, misleading), as the comparison attack strategy against the paper's own novel LLM-assisted iterative attack
